title: Meta's glasses are popular, but controversial, Kylie's debut shows
excerpt: Meta sold at least 7 million of its glasses last year, and Kylie Jenner debuted a new pair she designed. The backlash shows they're controversial.
content_html:
Kylie Jenner introduced her new Meta glasses. The backlash began.

By Katie Notopoulos

You're currently following this author! Want to unfollow? Unsubscribe via the link in your email.

Senior Correspondent covering technology and culture

Kylie Jenner is very much like Meta's glasses: You can't deny they're popular — but that doesn't mean they're not also controversial. (Just ask Club Chalamet. Actually, don't.)

It's undeniable that the camera-and-video smart glasses have sold — at least 7 million pairs last year. Now, a fresh wave of attention — and scrutiny — has followed the launch of a new style of the glasses: an oval-framed pair designed by Jenner.

When she debuted them late last month, there was an immediate backlash. One person commented, "Predator glasses" on her Instagram announcement. Another said. "Such a scary product for a WOMAN to be supporting." A British advocacy group bought a bus ad mocking the glasses with Jenner's image and the slogan "we're always watching you." I've reached out to Jenner's team for comment.

The anti-AI glasses sentiment might be getting louder simply because the glasses are getting more popular: They're a thing young people actually encounter out in the world. (I wrote about "Computah" back in June; he uses Meta's more traditional Ray-Bans to make funny videos pretending to "program" people.) They can also come with the potential creep factor. There's a whole genre of pick-up artists who post videos using them. I wrote about other people who do obnoxious pranks on service workers with the glasses and post on TikTok and Reels.

And at a Madrid music festival last week, the pop star Lorde went on an expletive-laden diatribe about them, saying "Don't get the glasses. Not sexy."

Interestingly, since the launch of her namesake frames, Jenner doesn't seem to be wearing them much in public. At the World Cup finals, she wore what appeared to be different tapered oval sunnies, and at a Knicks game, she used an old-school point-and-shoot camera to take photos of her boyfriend, rather than Meta glasses.

She did wear them in a recent Instagram post promoting her swimwear line.

The glasses, which can record video and take hands-free photos, have always been controversial — how could camera glasses not be? But it feels to me like we're at a new level of the discourse about them, where young people are both bemoaning their impact on society and also buying them.

What seems new here is the AI element, which is being beefed up in the glasses. In general, there's widespread anti-AI sentiment among young people, yet they continue to use AI. (Life is complicated; no judgment.)

Of course, the most controversial AI element in the glasses is one that doesn't exist on them (yet): facial recognition. Meta has been dancing around this feature for years. Whether it actually happens could depend on public reception — so it matters right now how Meta and its executives are messaging it.

That's where Meta's recent messaging around the glasses comes in.

NameTag would identify people you know

It's a relatable dilemma: You're talking to someone at a party, and you can't for the life of you remember their name. Or, even more brutal, you forget them completely, and they inform you that you've met before (oof).

This social agony is what Meta says it aims to solve with facial recognition in its AI-powered smart glasses. It hasn't launched facial recognition, but it seems to be very interested in doing so. Wired reported that a recent software update for the glasses included code (that wasn't actually working yet) for a feature called NameTag that could help you identify people you've met before.

But is the "cocktail party problem," as Meta CTO Andrew Bosworth called it in a recent interview on Nicholas Thompson's podcast, a big enough problem that we should be willing to make the privacy tradeoffs of a world where facial recognition glasses at parties are normalized?

A Meta spokesman told me facial recognition isn't yet a done deal.

"We haven't made a decision on the so-called NameTag feature, and it's not available in glasses people can buy today," Meta's Carl Woog said. "Should we decide to make something like this available, it will be our responsibility to do so with a thoughtful approach that protects people's privacy."

It feels hard to square the idea of facial recognition in glasses with personal privacy.

In the podcast interview, Bosworth also discusses the benefits of the glasses to the blind, as well as people who have trouble remembering faces or names because of a brain injury or other cognitive issue.

Facial recognition could have real benefits for the blind

That podcast interview wasn't the first time Meta executives or other company communications mentioned the glasses' disability benefits as a talking point.

I believe the people working on the glasses at Meta really care about the potential benefits for people with disabilities. The division at Meta that makes the glasses, Reality Labs, has its origins in Meta's acquisition of CTRL Labs, a company working on neural interface wearables meant to help people who couldn't type. In June, Meta pledged to provide free glasses to any of the 130,000 blind military veterans who request them.

"There are a lot of people working at Reality Labs who have dedicated their professional careers to building frontier technology that can help people who have real needs, be it limited sight, hearing, or mobility, and we are inspired by these efforts," Woog said.

Still, Meta glasses are not primarily sold as disability aids — they're fun toys for general consumers. And the discussion around disabilities also creates a kind of false tension: If you're worried about the privacy implications of facial recognition glasses, you might be against helping the blind. I have to believe there exists a middle ground; you can care about both things.

Meta knows that public sentiment is crucial to getting widespread acceptance of AI features like facial recognition. For now, it seems it will have to work on its messaging if the recent backlash to the Kylie glasses is any indication.

Read next

Katie Notopoulos

You're currently following this author! Want to unfollow? Unsubscribe via the link in your email.

Katie Notopoulos is a senior correspondent at Business Insider who writes about technology, business, and culture. She covers topics such as internet culture, Big Tech, retail, AI, parenting in the digital age, and personal tech.Previously, Katie was a tech reporter at BuzzFeed News and has written for The Atlantic, The New York Times, Fast Company, and MIT Technology Review. Based in New York, you can reach her by email knotopoulos@businessinsider.com or find her on Twitter. Bluesky, and Threads @katienotopoulos.Some of her stories include:

  • Google AI said to put glue in pizza — so I made a pizza with glue and ate it
  • The Zuckermoon is over
  • Gen Z doesn't want to say "hello" when answering the phone. I'm concerned. 
  • Wait, is Walmart cool now?
  • Mark Zuckerberg has created the saddest place on the internet with Meta AI's public feed
  • How Instagram got its mojo back
  • Am I the JD Vance of my group chat?
  • We need to talk about whatever's happening with Starbucks' drinks
  • This chart shows a key reason why millennial parents are miserable
  • It's not just you. Eggshells really are chipping more.

sentences.en: "We haven't made a decision on the so-called NameTag feature, and it's not available in glasses people can buy today," Meta's Carl Woog said.
sentences.zh: Meta 的卡尔·伍格 (Carl Woog) 表示：“我们还没有就所谓的 NameTag 功能做出决定，而且人们现在可以购买的眼镜中也没有该功能。”

sentences.en: "Should we decide to make something like this available, it will be our responsibility to do so with a thoughtful approach that protects people's privacy."
sentences.zh: “如果我们决定提供类似的服务，我们就有责任采取深思熟虑的方法来保护人们的隐私。”

blocks.html_en: It feels hard to square the idea of facial recognition in glasses with personal privacy.

sentences.en: It feels hard to square the idea of facial recognition in glasses with personal privacy.
sentences.zh: 感觉很难将眼镜中的面部识别与个人隐私联系起来。

blocks.html_en: In the podcast interview, Bosworth also discusses the benefits of the glasses to the blind, as well as people who have trouble remembering faces or names because of a brain injury or other cognitive issue.

sentences.en: In the podcast interview, Bosworth also discusses the benefits of the glasses to the blind, as well as people who have trouble remembering faces or names because of a brain injury or other cognitive issue.
sentences.zh: 在播客采访中，博斯沃思还讨论了眼镜对盲人以及因脑损伤或其他认知问题而难以记住面孔或名字的人的好处。

blocks.html_en: Facial recognition could have real benefits for the blind

sentences.en: Facial recognition could have real benefits for the blind
sentences.zh: 面部识别可以为盲人带来真正的好处

blocks.html_en: That podcast interview wasn't the first time Meta executives or other company communications mentioned the glasses' disability benefits as a talking point.

sentences.en: That podcast interview wasn't the first time Meta executives or other company communications mentioned the glasses' disability benefits as a talking point.
sentences.zh: 那次播客采访并不是 Meta 高管或其他公司通讯第一次提到眼镜的残疾福利作为话题。

blocks.html_en: I believe the people working on the glasses at Meta really care about the potential benefits for people with disabilities. The division at Meta that makes the glasses, Reality Labs, has its origins in Meta's acquisition of CTRL Labs, a company working on neural interface wearables meant to help people who couldn't type. In June, Meta pledged to provide free glasses to any of the 130,000 blind military veterans who request them.

sentences.en: I believe the people working on the glasses at Meta really care about the potential benefits for people with disabilities.
sentences.zh: 我相信 Meta 眼镜研发人员确实关心残疾人士的潜在利益。

sentences.en: The division at Meta that makes the glasses, Reality Labs, has its origins in Meta's acquisition of CTRL Labs, a company working on neural interface wearables meant to help people who couldn't type.
sentences.zh: Meta 生产眼镜的部门 Reality Labs 起源于 Meta 收购 CTRL Labs，后者是一家致力于帮助无法打字的人开发神经接口可穿戴设备的公司。

sentences.en: In June, Meta pledged to provide free glasses to any of the 130,000 blind military veterans who request them.
sentences.zh: 6 月，Meta 承诺为 13 万名有需要的盲人退伍军人提供免费眼镜。

blocks.html_en: "There are a lot of people working at Reality Labs who have dedicated their professional careers to building frontier technology that can help people who have real needs, be it limited sight, hearing, or mobility, and we are inspired by these efforts," Woog said.

sentences.en: "There are a lot of people working at Reality Labs who have dedicated their professional careers to building frontier technology that can help people who have real needs, be it limited sight, hearing, or mobility, and we are inspired by these efforts," Woog said.
sentences.zh: 伍格说：“现实实验室有很多人将他们的职业生涯奉献给了构建前沿技术，帮助那些有实际需求的人，无论是视力、听力还是行动不便的人，我们都受到了这些努力的启发。”

blocks.html_en: Still, Meta glasses are not primarily sold as disability aids — they're fun toys for general consumers. And the discussion around disabilities also creates a kind of false tension: If you're worried about the privacy implications of facial recognition glasses, you might be against helping the blind. I have to believe there exists a middle ground; you can care about both things.

sentences.en: Still, Meta glasses are not primarily sold as disability aids — they're fun toys for general consumers.
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，Meta 眼镜主要并不是作为残疾辅助工具出售——它们对于普通消费者来说是有趣的玩具。

sentences.en: And the discussion around disabilities also creates a kind of false tension: If you're worried about the privacy implications of facial recognition glasses, you might be against helping the blind.
sentences.zh: 关于残疾的讨论也造成了一种虚假的紧张：如果你担心面部识别眼镜对隐私的影响，你可能会反对帮助盲人。

sentences.en: I have to believe there exists a middle ground; you can care about both things.
sentences.zh: 我必须相信存在一个中间立场；你可以关心这两件事。

blocks.html_en: Meta knows that public sentiment is crucial to getting widespread acceptance of AI features like facial recognition. For now, it seems it will have to work on its messaging if the recent backlash to the Kylie glasses is any indication.

sentences.en: Meta knows that public sentiment is crucial to getting widespread acceptance of AI features like facial recognition.
sentences.zh: Meta 知道公众情绪对于面部识别等人工智能功能的广泛接受至关重要。

sentences.en: For now, it seems it will have to work on its messaging if the recent backlash to the Kylie glasses is any indication.
sentences.zh: 就目前而言，如果最近对凯莉眼镜的强烈反对有任何迹象的话，它似乎必须努力传达信息。

blocks.html_en: Katie Notopoulos is a senior correspondent at Business Insider who writes about technology, business, and culture. She covers topics such as internet culture, Big Tech, retail, AI, parenting in the digital age, and personal tech.Previously, Katie was a tech reporter at BuzzFeed News and has written for The Atlantic, The New York Times, Fast Company, and MIT Technology Review. Based in New York, you can reach her by email knotopoulos@businessinsider.com or find her on Twitter. Bluesky, and Threads @katienotopoulos.Some of her stories include:

content_html_zh:
凯莉·詹纳 (Kylie Jenner) 推出了她的新款 Meta 眼镜。 反弹开始了。

作者：Katie Notopoulos 您目前正在关注该作者！ 想要取消关注吗？ 通过电子邮件中的链接取消订阅。 资深记者报道科技与文化

凯莉·詹纳 (Kylie Jenner) 与 Meta 的眼镜非常相似：你不能否认它们很受欢迎，但这并不意味着它们没有争议。 （问问查拉梅俱乐部就知道了。 事实上，不要。）

不可否认的是，这款摄像头和视频智能眼镜去年的销量至少达到了 700 万副。 现在，随着一种新型眼镜的推出，新一波的关注和审视随之而来：由詹纳设计的椭圆形镜框眼镜。

当她上个月末首次亮相时，立即引起了强烈反对。 一位用户在她的 Instagram 声明中评论道：“Predator 眼镜”。 另一个说。 “对于一个女人来说，支持这样的产品真是太可怕了。” 一个英国倡导团体购买了一个公交车广告，用詹纳的形象和口号“我们一直在看着你”来嘲笑眼镜。 我已联系詹纳的团队征求意见。

反人工智能眼镜的情绪可能会越来越高涨，仅仅是因为眼镜变得越来越流行：它们是年轻人在现实世界中实际遇到的东西。 （我早在 6 月份就写过关于“Computah”的文章；他使用 Meta 更传统的雷朋眼镜制作有趣的视频，假装对人们进行“编程”。） 它们还可以具有潜在的蠕变系数。 有很多把妹艺术家都用它们来发布视频。 我写过其他人对戴着眼镜的服务人员进行令人讨厌的恶作剧，并在 TikTok 和 Reels 上发帖。

在上周的马德里音乐节上，流行歌星洛德对他们进行了充满咒骂的谩骂，说“别拿眼镜。 不性感。”

有趣的是，自从推出同名镜框以来，詹纳似乎很少在公共场合佩戴它们。 在世界杯决赛上，她戴着看似不同的锥形椭圆形太阳镜，在尼克斯队的比赛中，她使用老式傻瓜相机给男友拍照，而不是梅塔眼镜。

她在最近的 Instagram 帖子中确实穿着它们宣传她的泳装系列。

这款可以录制视频和免提拍照的眼镜一直备受争议——拍照眼镜怎么可能不被争议呢？ 但在我看来，我们对它们的讨论正处于一个新的水平，年轻人既哀叹它们对社会的影响，又购买它们。

这里似乎新的是人工智能元素，它在眼镜中得到了加强。 总体而言，年轻人中普遍存在反人工智能情绪，但他们仍在继续使用人工智能。 （生活很复杂；没有判断力。）

当然，眼镜中最具争议的人工智能元素是眼镜上尚不存在的元素：面部识别。 Meta 多年来一直围绕这个功能进行研究。 它是否真的发生可能取决于公众的接受程度——所以现在 Meta 及其高管如何传达这一信息很重要。

这就是 Meta 最近关于眼镜的信息的来源。

姓名标签可以识别您认识的人

这是一个相关的困境：你正在聚会上与某人交谈，但你却一辈子都记不住他们的名字。 或者，更残酷的是，你完全忘记了他们，他们告诉你你以前见过（oof）。

Meta 表示，它的目标是通过人工智能智能眼镜中的面部识别来解决这种社交痛苦。 它尚未推出面部识别功能，但似乎对此非常感兴趣。 据《连线》报道，这款眼镜最近的软件更新包含了名为 NameTag 的功能的代码（实际上尚未运行），该功能可以帮助您识别以前见过的人。

但是，Meta 首席技术官 Andrew Bosworth 在最近接受 Nicholas Thompson 播客采访时所称的“鸡尾酒会问题”是否是一个足够大的问题，以至于我们应该愿意在派对上的面部识别眼镜正常化的世界中做出隐私权衡？

Meta 的一位发言人告诉我，面部识别尚未敲定。

Meta 的卡尔·伍格 (Carl Woog) 表示：“我们还没有就所谓的 NameTag 功能做出决定，而且人们现在可以购买的眼镜中也没有该功能。” “如果我们决定提供类似的服务，我们就有责任采取深思熟虑的方法来保护人们的隐私。”

感觉很难将眼镜中的面部识别与个人隐私联系起来。

在播客采访中，博斯沃思还讨论了眼镜对盲人以及因脑损伤或其他认知问题而难以记住面孔或名字的人的好处。

面部识别可以为盲人带来真正的好处

那次播客采访并不是 Meta 高管或其他公司通讯第一次提到眼镜的残疾福利作为话题。

我相信 Meta 眼镜研发人员确实关心残疾人士的潜在利益。 Meta 生产眼镜的部门 Reality Labs 起源于 Meta 收购 CTRL Labs，后者是一家致力于帮助无法打字的人开发神经接口可穿戴设备的公司。 6 月，Meta 承诺为 13 万名有需要的盲人退伍军人提供免费眼镜。

伍格说：“现实实验室有很多人将他们的职业生涯奉献给了构建前沿技术，帮助那些有实际需求的人，无论是视力、听力还是行动不便的人，我们都受到了这些努力的启发。”

尽管如此，Meta 眼镜主要并不是作为残疾辅助工具出售——它们对于普通消费者来说是有趣的玩具。 关于残疾的讨论也造成了一种虚假的紧张：如果你担心面部识别眼镜对隐私的影响，你可能会反对帮助盲人。 我必须相信存在一个中间立场；你可以关心这两件事。

Meta 知道公众情绪对于面部识别等人工智能功能的广泛接受至关重要。 就目前而言，如果最近对凯莉眼镜的强烈反对有任何迹象的话，它似乎必须努力传达信息。

阅读下一篇
